english: dressed to one going on a journey, such as: good success! may all go well! — 2. modest, attentive to the wishes of others.

(Lex. दण्ड punishment) 1. punishment, chastisement for sins, visitation; in this sense the word is said to be used still, but much more frq. it signifies 2. tax, tribute, duty, service to be performed to a higher master; Kral (khral) sdú-ba (sdu ba) to collect taxes, jál-ba ('jal ba), skór-ba (skor ba) to pay taxes, bkál-ba (bkal ba) to impose taxes; dňúl-kral (dngul khral) money-tax, tax to be paid in money, brú-kral ('bru khral) corn-tax, tribute paid in corn, til-már-kral (til mar khral) tax, tribute to be paid in sesame-oil.

(Cs. kri-ma (khri ma)), kri-krág (khri khrag), kri-tsó (khri tsho) ten thousand, a myriad, kri pyeḍ daň gnyis (khri phyed dang gnyis) 15 000; nyi-kri (nyi khri) 20 000; bżi-kri (bzhi khri) 40 000; brgyad-kri bżi-stoň (brgyad khri bzhi stong) 84 000, a number frq. occurring in legends.

also kriu (khri'u), seldom kri-bo (khri bo), resp. bẓúgs-kri (bzhugs khri), seat, chair; throne; couch; frame, sawing-jack, trestle etc.; *gya-ṭi* (gya ti) an Indian (Anglo-Indian, European) chair: čós-kri (chos khri) a professorial chair, pulpit Pth., reading-desk, table for books, school-table; *nyé-ṭi* (nye ti) (v. snyé-ba (snye ba)) a contrivance to rest the head on when sleeping on the ground W. nyál-kri (nyal khri), resp. gzím-kri (gzim khri), bed-stead; sén-ge-kri (seng ge khri) throne; kri-la (khri la) bskó-ba (bsko ba) to raise on the throne; kri-la (khri la) ḳód-pa ('khod pa) to preside, to hold the chair. — As the Tibetans generally sit on the bare ground, or on mats, or carpets, chairs are rather articles of luxury. Comp. and deriv. Kri-gdúgs (khri gdugs) po. the sun. — Kri-pa (khri pa) Cs. a chairman; one sitting on a throne. — kri-pȧn (khri 'phan) 1. Cs.: the height of a chair, a high chair. — 2. mčod-rtén-gyi (mchod rten gyi) kri-pȧn (khri 'phan) the same as baň-rím (bang rim). — kri-mún (khri mun) or món (mon) Pth., Tar., prison, dungeon. — *ṭi-šiň* (ti shing), *ṭiu-šiň* (ti'u shing), the common, plain word for chair.
1. so krig-krig (khrig khrig) byéḍ-pa (byed pa) to gnash, grind the teeth Mil.; gzugs-po (gzugs po) to shiver, shake with cold, terror, rage Mil. — 2. col. for tig-tig (thig thig).

plentiful, abundant Sch.; za-óg (za 'og) Krigs-se (khrigs se) silk-fabrics, silks, in abundance Mil.; krigs-se (khrigs se) gaň (gang) quite full Sch.; krigs-se (khrigs se) byéḍ-pa (byed pa) to treat, to entertain plentifully Sch.

instruction, teaching; krid (khrid) ḍébs-pa ('debs pa) to give instruction, to instruct, krid-pas-čog (khrid pas chog) I am willing to give you instruction, you may have lessons with me Mil.; krid (khrid) bšáḍ-pa (bshad pa) to give instruction, to make admonitory speeches, to give parenetical lectures; krid (khrid) záb-po (zab po) thorough instruction; slu-kriḍ (slu khrid) instruction to an evil purpose, seduction, v. slu-ba (slu ba); sna-kriḍ (sna khrid) Lex. guide, leader. — krid-mkán (khrid mkhan) col. teacher. — krid-prúg (khrid phrug) scholar, pupil. — krid-pa (khrid pa) v. ḳrid-pa ('khrid pa).

1. right, not in the abstract sense in which the word is generally understood with us, but in more or less concrete applications, such as administration of justice, law, judgment, sometimes also implying custom, usage, duty. Accordingly rgyál-po (rgyal po), or btsún-po (btsun po) krims-méḍ (khrims med) means an unjust king, an unprincipled priest or ecclesiastic; krims (khrims) bżin-du (bzhin du), krims (khrims) daň (dang) mtun-par (mthun par) conscientiously, justly; in conformity with custom, duty, law; čos-krims (chos khrims) religious right, coming nearest to our abstract right; when, for instance, in Glr. king Sroň-btsan-sgam-po (srong btsan sgam po) says: rgyál-krims (rgyal khrims) čós-krims-su (chos khrims su) bsgyur (bsgyur) I have changed the right of a king into that of religion, he means to say: I have subjected my own absolute will to the higher principle of universal right. A somewhat different sense conveys Glr. 97, 4: čós-krims (chos khrims) ẓig-pai (zhig pa'i) gros (gros) byas (byas) they conspired to extirpate the religious principle of administration. — 2. law, dgé-ba (dge ba) bču (bcu) daň (dang) ldan-pai (ldan pa'i) krims (khrims) a general law, founded upon the ten virtues Glr.; des (des) čós-krims (chos khrims) daň (dang) rgyál-krims (rgyal khrims) gnyis (gnyis) ytán-la (gtan la) páb (phab), he regulated the spiritual and secular law Glr. 97, 1.; bka-krims (bka' khrims) resp. law, as a collection of precepts, decree, commandment; krims (khrims) čá-ba (bca' ba) to enact a law, to publish a decree, frq.; krims (khrims) sgróg-pa (sgrog pa) to pro-
